quotes.verbatim_quote: Note: This pattern utilizes the chord scale fragment 1-3-5-3.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names and defines the specific chord-scale fragment that patterns 52 and 53 are built from, establishing the method's systematic approach to arpeggiating chords through scale-degree sequences.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Note: This pattern utilizes the chord scale fragment 5-3-1-3.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines an inverted version of the core fragment, showing how the method generates new patterns by reordering the same scale degrees.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Note: This pattern utilizes the chord scale fragment 3-5-2-1.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces a fragment that extends beyond triad tones to include the 2nd scale degree, showing how non-harmonic tones are incorporated into the patterns.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern utilizes two fragments (5-8-6-4 and 2-5-3-1) from the chord scale, which when played simultaneously will sound any of the major forms of the chords expressed (Triad, M6, M7 and M9).
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the key theoretical payoff of combining two fragments: a single pattern covers all major chord qualities (triad through M9), making it a load-bearing generalization rule.
